note: This record does not modify any PR #79/#80 file, does not re-encode frame 004, and does not authorize Runtime Integration itself. It records the result of an isolated, test-only compatibility check.

targetAsset.pathResolutionNote: Task prompt's stated path ('.../frames/004.png') does not exist in the repository; resolved from PR #79/#80's own records to the actual file, which follows the established naming convention hero.archer_attack_v3_2_<NNN>.png.

toolchainDiscovery.productionTargetDecoderPath: Browser-native PNG decoder (Blink/Skia for Chromium-family browsers). The game's entry point (autochess.html) loads THREE.js r128 via a classic <script> tag from cdnjs.cloudflare.com, plus src/game.js (also a classic script, not an ES module). src/game.js uses `new THREE.TextureLoader()` (see loadAllSprites(), src/game.js:364) to load all sprite assets.
toolchainDiscovery.threeJsTextureLoaderInternals: THREE r128's TextureLoader has no PNG decoder of its own. Internally (via THREE.ImageLoader) it creates a native `Image`, sets `.src` to the asset URL, and on load wraps the resulting HTMLImageElement in a THREE.Texture object. THREE.WebGLRenderer then uploads that texture via the native WebGL `texImage2D` API on first render. There is no THREE-specific or JavaScript-implemented image decoding step -- the browser's built-in decoder does 100% of the PNG-to-bitmap work.
toolchainDiscovery.existingTestableLocalDecoderPath: Headless Chromium via Playwright (globally installed at /opt/node22/lib/node_modules/playwright; browser binaries pre-installed at /opt/pw-browsers, PLAYWRIGHT_BROWSERS_PATH already set). This is the same Blink/Skia engine family that would run the production CDN build in any Chromium-based browser.
toolchainDiscovery.cannotLoadActualThreeJsR128: Outbound network access to cdnjs.cloudflare.com (and unpkg.com, tested as a fallback) is blocked by this environment's proxy policy (curl: CONNECT tunnel failed, response 403). No vendored/local copy of three.js exists anywhere in the repository. As a result, THREE.js r128's own code was not executed in this check.
toolchainDiscovery.whyThisIsStillRepresentative: Because THREE.TextureLoader is a thin wrapper over native `Image` decode + native WebGL `texImage2D` upload (see threeJsTextureLoaderInternals above), this check exercises those two primitives directly in the same browser engine, which determines 100% of whether a 16-bit PNG decodes and uploads correctly. This is NOT identical to running THREE.js r128's own code path, and is explicitly disclosed as a limitation below -- but it is not merely 'ImageMagick/Pillow only' either: it is real browser-engine decode + real WebGL texture upload.
runtimeRequirementsIdentified: Decode must succeed (no onerror / decode exception)
Alpha channel must be correctly preserved after decode
Visual output (sampled pixel RGBA values) must match an independently-decoded reference
Texture upload via WebGL texImage2D must succeed with no GL error
No memory/format anomaly that would cause a visible defect or throw during a full 12-frame animation sequence load
limitationsNotTestableInThisEnvironment: THREE.js r128's own TextureLoader/Texture/WebGLRenderer code paths were not executed (network-blocked CDN, no vendored copy). Native Image decode and native WebGL texImage2D -- the two primitives THREE actually delegates to -- were tested directly instead.

Only Chromium/Blink was tested. WebKit (Safari/iOS) and Gecko (Firefox) engines were not tested in this environment; if the target audience includes significant non-Chromium mobile/desktop browser share, this remains an open (though likely low-risk, since 16-bit PNG is part of the PNG spec baseline and all three engines support decoding it) gap.

No real GPU/driver combination beyond this container's software/virtual GL implementation (WebGL 2.0 (OpenGL ES 3.0 Chromium), per gl.getParameter(gl.VERSION) in the test) was exercised. Real end-user GPU/driver diversity was not tested.

This check tests asset-level decode/upload in isolation via a local static file server, not the actual production hosting (GitHub Pages, per the deploy workflow referenced elsewhere in this repo) or the running game's actual sprite-sheet frame-slicing/animation-cycling logic.

binaryIntegrity.method: Python struct-level PNG chunk walk + zlib.crc32 verification, run directly against the exact repository binary

browserDecodeTest.method: tools/test-archer-attack-v3-2-16bit-compatibility-v1.mjs -- Playwright-driven headless Chromium, native `new Image()` decode via a local static HTTP server (loopback, matching the http(s) delivery mechanism the real game uses, not file://)
browserDecodeTest.harnessNote: The harness required one fix during this check: Chromium's Private Network Access policy blocks a page with a null/opaque origin (about:blank) from fetching loopback (127.0.0.1) resources. Navigating the page to a real http:// origin first (before the compatibility fetch/decode calls) resolved this. This was an environment/harness issue unrelated to the 16-bit PNG itself, confirmed by first reproducing and isolating it with a minimal standalone script before concluding it was not a format-compatibility problem.

neighborComparison.note: Both 8-bit neighbor frames decode via the identical code path with no observable behavioral difference from the 16-bit frame 004 -- same success pattern, same dimension resolution, same alpha-bounds measurement method.

sequenceLoadTest.note: All 12 frames (000-011), including the 16-bit frame 004, decode successfully in sequence with no exceptions, no load failures, and no dimension drift.

pixelCrossVerification.method: Independently decoded the same pixel (320,480) via Pillow (Python) and compared to the browser's decoded value
pixelCrossVerification.conclusion: No channel swap, no inversion, no color corruption introduced by the browser's internal 16-bit-to-8-bit-canvas-backing-store downsampling.

webglTextureUploadTest.method: Native WebGL texImage2D call (same API THREE.WebGLRenderer/Texture uses internally on first render), from within the same Playwright/Chromium session, on the decoded frame 004 HTMLImageElement
webglTextureUploadTest.disposeNote: gl.deleteTexture() correctly invalidated the texture handle, mirroring the eventual effect of THREE.Texture.dispose().

existingCandidateValidator.note: Re-run in this worktree to confirm no drift; still reports the same non-blocking 16-bit anomaly for frame 004, unchanged.

existingApprovalValidator.note: Re-run in this worktree to confirm no drift in the approval record.

combatX4.reason: No Runtime/Combat integration performed or started in this check; Combat x4 testing is out of scope.

comparisonAgainst8BitNeighbor.decodePath: identical (native Image decode, same code path, same success/failure signaling)
comparisonAgainst8BitNeighbor.dimensions: identical resolution behavior (640x960 for all three)
comparisonAgainst8BitNeighbor.channelRepresentation: identical after decode -- browser canvas ImageData is always Uint8ClampedArray RGBA regardless of source PNG bit depth; 16-bit source is transparently downsampled to 8-bit during decode, same as any browser would do for any 16-bit PNG per the PNG/HTML Canvas spec
comparisonAgainst8BitNeighbor.alphaInterpretation: identical -- straight (non-premultiplied) alpha correctly preserved in all three, confirmed via corner (transparent) and center (opaque) samples

fileSizeApprox.note: frame 004 is ~2.5x larger on disk due to 16-bit encoding, but this is a one-time network-transfer/storage cost, not a decode-time or runtime-memory behavioral difference -- decoded in-memory representation is 8-bit RGBA for all three once rasterized to canvas/texture.

comparisonAgainst8BitNeighbor.textureCreationAndUpload: identical WebGL upload path exercised for frame 004; not independently re-tested for 003/005 in this check since the upload mechanism is not bit-depth-dependent (same texImage2D call signature regardless of source PNG bit depth once the browser has decoded to a bitmap)
comparisonAgainst8BitNeighbor.conclusion: No behavioral difference detected between the 16-bit frame 004 and its 8-bit neighbors at any stage of the tested pipeline.

visualPixelValidation.note: Visual pixel validation performed via programmatic sampling (corner/center/bbox-corner points) plus cross-decoder pixel-value comparison, not human eyeball review -- human visual review of frame 004 already occurred and was approved in PR #80; this check re-confirms the decoded pixels are byte-consistent with that approved content, not a re-review of artistic quality.

classification.reason: Exact binary verified against expected SHA-256 and PNG properties. Production-equivalent decode primitives (native browser Image decode, the same mechanism THREE r128's TextureLoader delegates to) succeeded with correct dimensions, correct alpha bounds (exact match to expected), and pixel values cross-verified against an independent decoder with zero discrepancy. WebGL texImage2D texture upload -- the same API THREE.WebGLRenderer uses -- succeeded with zero GL errors and correct lifecycle (create/upload/dispose). All 12 frames in the sequence, including frame 004, decode without exception or dimension drift. No evidence of any 16-bit-caused failure at any tested layer. The one real limitation (THREE.js r128 itself not executed, due to network-blocked CDN) is disclosed explicitly and is judged non-critical because the primitives THREE delegates to for exactly this concern were tested directly in the same browser engine.

nextGateNote: This gate is not started by this record. Runtime Integration work itself -- registering frame 004 (and the full v3.2 12-frame sequence) into src/game.js's asset pipeline, wiring animation playback, and any associated Combat/marker changes -- requires a separate, explicitly scoped task and is not authorized to begin as a side effect of this compatibility check passing.
